Drift monitoring: In production, how wou: Data drift (input distribution shifting) is detected by comparing feature statistics against a training baseline using tests like population stability index; concept drift (the relationship between inputs and target changing) shows up as accuracy degradation on labeled feedback data even if inputs look stable; schema drift (fields changing type, appearing, or disappearing) is caught by validating incoming data against an expected schema before it reaches the model. Data and schema drift typically trigger a pipeline alert and pause, while concept drift triggers a retraining evaluation, since it usually means the underlying real-world pattern has genuinely changed.
Model registry: How would you design mod: Register every trained model with its version, training data reference, code commit, and evaluation metrics attached as metadata, and gate promotion from staging to production stages behind an explicit approval (automated threshold check, human sign-off, or both) rather than direct registration. Keep prior production versions in the registry so rollback is pointing the serving endpoint at a previous version, and ensure lineage links every deployed model back to the exact training run and data that produced it for audit purposes.
Feature store: What production risks doe: A feature store solves training/serving skew (features computed differently for training versus real-time inference) and duplicated feature engineering logic across teams. Design freshness with defined SLAs per feature (batch-updated daily versus streaming near-real-time), enforce parity by using the same feature computation code path for both training and serving, add governance via feature ownership and access control since features often contain sensitive data, and support rollback by versioning feature definitions so a bad feature change can be reverted without breaking dependent models.
Batch vs real-time inference: How would: Use batch inference when predictions are needed on a schedule and latency doesn't matter (nightly churn scoring); real-time inference when a user is waiting for a response (fraud check during checkout); asynchronous inference when the request can be queued and the result delivered later (document processing); and streaming inference when predictions need to update continuously as new events arrive (real-time anomaly detection on a data stream). The choice drives very different infrastructure - batch favors cost-efficient scheduled jobs, real-time demands low-latency always-on serving.
A/B and canary for ML: How would you run: Start with a canary (small percentage of traffic) to catch outright failures or latency regressions before wider exposure, then run a proper A/B test with randomized, statistically sized cohorts to measure actual business impact (not just model accuracy) against the incumbent model. Define the success metric and minimum sample size before starting to avoid peeking bias, and always have a kill switch to revert to the previous model immediately if the new model shows harmful behavior mid-test.
Model rollback: A newly deployed model h: Detect it by monitoring business metrics (conversion, revenue, user engagement) alongside technical metrics, not just latency and accuracy, since a model can be technically healthy while quietly hurting the business. Roll back by repointing the serving endpoint to the previous registered model version - this should be a fast, low-risk operation if the registry and deployment pipeline were designed for it - and investigate offline why the new model underperformed on business outcomes despite passing technical evaluation before attempting to redeploy it.
Reproducible training: How would you mak: Pin the code version (git commit), data version (a versioned dataset snapshot or a query with a fixed timestamp, not a live table), the exact feature computation logic, and the training environment (containerized with pinned dependency versions) for every run. Capture all of this as metadata alongside the resulting model artifact in the registry, so any past model version can be traced back to and, if needed, regenerated from the exact same inputs.
Metadata management: What metadata would: Capture the code commit, data version/snapshot reference, hyperparameters, training environment, evaluation metrics, the person or pipeline that triggered the run, and the resulting model artifact's location and hash. This metadata is what makes rollback safe (know exactly what you're rolling back to), debugging possible (reproduce the conditions that produced a bad model), and compliance auditable (prove what data and code produced a decision-making model).
Champion-challenger: How would you imple: Deploy the challenger model alongside the champion, routing a small, controlled percentage of live traffic to the challenger while the champion continues serving the majority, and log both models' predictions on the same requests where possible for direct comparison. Evaluate the challenger against the champion on the same business metrics over a defined period before promoting it to champion status, and keep the previous champion available to revert to instantly if the challenger underperforms once fully promoted.
Kubernetes control plane: Explain what h: kubectl sends the manifest to the API server, which validates and persists it to etcd; a controller (e.g. Deployment controller) notices the new desired state and creates a Pod object; the scheduler watches for unscheduled pods and assigns one to a suitable node based on resource requests and constraints. The kubelet on that node sees the assignment, pulls the container image, and instructs the container runtime to start the containers, while the CNI plugin assigns the pod an IP and wires up networking so it can communicate within the cluster.
StatefulSet vs Deployment: When would yo: Use Deployment for stateless, interchangeable replicas (most web services); StatefulSet when pods need stable network identity and persistent storage tied to a specific pod ordinal (databases, message queues); Job for a run-to-completion task; CronJob for scheduled recurring tasks; and DaemonSet when exactly one pod must run per node (log collectors, node-level monitoring agents). Choosing the wrong primitive - like using a Deployment for a database - typically causes data consistency or identity problems under scaling or rescheduling.
CNI troubleshooting: Pods on different n: Start by checking whether the CNI plugin's pods (e.g. Calico, Cilium daemonset) are healthy on both nodes, since a CNI agent crash on one node breaks all pod networking there. Check node routing tables to confirm pod CIDR routes exist between nodes, verify no firewall rule is blocking the pod network CIDR range at the VPC level, check for a NetworkPolicy inadvertently blocking the traffic, and confirm the nodes themselves are Ready and not under network-affecting resource pressure.
Pod affinity: When would you use node se: Use node selectors/affinity to require or prefer scheduling on nodes with specific characteristics (GPU nodes, a specific zone); anti-affinity to spread replicas apart so a single node or zone failure doesn't take down all instances of a service; topology spread constraints for more precise, weighted distribution across zones/nodes than affinity alone provides; and taints/tolerations to reserve nodes for specific workloads (GPU nodes tainted so only GPU pods schedule there) while keeping general workloads off them.
Resource quotas: How would you design Re: Set ResourceQuotas per namespace based on each team's actual expected usage plus reasonable headroom, reviewed periodically rather than set once and forgotten, and use LimitRanges to enforce sane default requests/limits so a pod deployed without explicit resource specs doesn't either starve or monopolize the node. Provide a clear, fast process for teams to request a quota increase when they have a legitimate scaling need, so the quota protects the cluster without becoming a bottleneck that teams route around.
Docker image design: How would you build: Use multi-stage builds so build tools and intermediate artifacts never reach the final image, pin the base image to a specific digest (not a floating tag) for reproducibility, and run as a non-root user with a minimal or distroless final image to reduce attack surface. Scan images for vulnerabilities in CI before they can be pushed to a registry consumed by production, and keep image layers ordered so dependency installs (rarely changing) come before application code (frequently changing) to maximize build cache efficiency.
Helm rollback: A Helm upgrade failed and: Use `helm rollback <release> <revision>` to revert to the last known-good release, then investigate what specifically failed (a bad values change, a resource that violates a policy, a hook failure) from the upgrade logs. Prevent recurrence by enabling `--atomic` on future upgrades so Helm automatically rolls back on failure instead of leaving a partial state, and add a pre-upgrade validation step (helm template + a policy check, or a dry-run against staging) before applying to production.
DevOps lifecycle: Explain how you would: Code is committed behind branch protection with required reviews, CI runs lint/test/build and produces an immutable versioned artifact, which is scanned for vulnerabilities before being promoted through environments (dev, staging, production) via the same artifact rather than rebuilding per stage. Deployment uses progressive delivery (canary or blue-green) with automated rollback on metric regression, and production operations close the loop with monitoring, alerting, on-call, and incident/postmortem processes that feed learnings back into the pipeline and codebase.
Branching strategy: What Git branching a: For application services with continuous deployment, trunk-based development with short-lived feature branches and feature flags for incomplete work usually moves fastest and avoids painful long-lived merge conflicts. For platform/Terraform modules consumed by many teams, use semantic versioning with release branches or tags so consumers can pin to a known-stable version and upgrade deliberately, since a breaking change in a shared module has a much larger blast radius than in a single application.
Artifact promotion: How would you promot: Build the artifact (container image, package) exactly once per commit with an immutable identifier, push it to a registry, and promote the same artifact through environments by updating a deployment reference (image tag/digest in a manifest) rather than triggering a new build per environment. Attach build provenance metadata (source commit, build system, signer) to the artifact so every environment can verify it's running exactly what was tested, not a rebuilt variant that could differ subtly.
Secrets in CI/CD: How would you prevent: Store secrets in each platform's native secret store (masked CI variables, or better, fetch from Secret Manager/Vault at runtime with short-lived credentials via OIDC) rather than hardcoding them in pipeline YAML or Jenkinsfiles. Ensure the CI platform masks secret values in logs automatically, restrict which branches/environments can access production secrets, and scan repositories and pipeline configs regularly for accidentally committed credentials.
Deployment rollback criteria: What metri: Automate rollback on clear technical regressions - error rate spike, latency p95/p99 breach, health check failures - and, where measurable in near-real-time, business signals like a sharp drop in conversion or checkout completion. Set thresholds based on statistical significance and a reasonable observation window (not the first data point) to avoid false-positive rollbacks from normal noise, and always allow a human override to halt an automated rollback if the team has context the automation doesn't.
Infrastructure testing: How would you te: Test Terraform modules with `terraform validate`, `plan` review, and tools like Terratest that actually provision and verify resources in an ephemeral test environment. Validate Kubernetes manifests and Helm charts with `kubeval`/`kubeconform` for schema correctness and `helm template` plus a policy check (OPA/Conftest) to catch misconfigurations before they reach a cluster. Run all of this in CI on every change so infrastructure changes get the same test rigor as application code before merging.
Disaster recovery testing: How would you: Design the test to simulate the real failure mode as closely as possible - actually fail over DNS, actually restore from backup into a separate environment, actually bring the application up and verify it serves correct data - rather than checking each component in isolation and assuming they work together. Measure actual time-to-recovery against the target RTO/RPO, and include a communication dry-run (who gets notified, in what order) as part of the test, since a DR plan that only tests technical recovery misses half of what makes an incident go smoothly.
Error budget policy: How would you creat: Define the policy before it's needed, not during a crisis: when the error budget is healthy, teams can ship freely and take reasonable risks; as the budget depletes, require extra review or testing for risky changes; and when it's exhausted, freeze non-critical feature releases and redirect effort to reliability work until the budget recovers. Make the policy automatic and visible (a dashboard showing current budget status) so it's a shared, objective signal rather than a judgment call made under pressure.
Capacity planning math: What inputs woul: Use historical peak usage trends (not averages) per resource dimension, expected growth rate from product/business forecasts, and headroom for burst traffic and node failures (N+1 or N+2 capacity so losing a node doesn't cause cascading failures). For regional failover, plan capacity assuming a full region's traffic could shift to another region, which typically means each region needs meaningfully more spare capacity than its own steady-state load would suggest.
Distributed tracing: A request crosses A: Propagate a single trace ID across every hop by instrumenting each service with OpenTelemetry and ensuring trace context headers are forwarded through synchronous calls and embedded in Pub/Sub message attributes for asynchronous hops (since trace context doesn't propagate automatically across a message queue). Export all spans to a single backend (Cloud Trace or an OTel collector feeding Jaeger/Tempo) so the full request path - including the async Pub/Sub leg - renders as one connected trace rather than disconnected fragments.
Security baseline: What minimum security: Enforce via org policy: no public IPs on VMs without explicit exception, Public Access Prevention on storage, required OS Login instead of SSH keys, restricted service account key creation (prefer Workload Identity), default-deny firewall with explicit allow rules, and mandatory audit logging enabled. Combine automated enforcement with a lightweight review for exceptions, so the baseline is consistent by default rather than dependent on every team remembering to configure it manually.
WAF exceptions: A Cloud Armor WAF rule b: Check Cloud Armor logs to see exactly which rule triggered and what request pattern matched, and confirm the traffic is genuinely legitimate (not an attacker who happens to resemble a false positive). Create a narrowly scoped exception - specific to the exact request pattern, source, or path rather than disabling the rule broadly - document why the exception exists and its expiry/review date, and monitor it afterward to confirm it isn't being abused as an unintended bypass.
Vulnerability management: How would you: Scan container images in CI before push and continuously in the registry (new CVEs are discovered after images are built), scan VM OS packages and dependencies with a tool integrated into the patching workflow, and track base image freshness since an outdated base image is a common source of accumulating vulnerabilities. Prioritize remediation by actual exploitability and exposure (internet-facing critical vulnerabilities first) rather than raw CVE count, and set SLAs by severity that are actually enforced and tracked to closure.
Network segmentation: How would you segm: Separate environments (dev/staging/prod) into different projects or at minimum different VPCs/subnets so a misconfiguration in dev cannot reach production, and use Shared VPC with service projects so teams get network isolation for their workloads while networking stays centrally governed. Apply firewall rules based on tags/service accounts rather than broad IP ranges, and use folder-level IAM and org policies so new projects inherit segmentation and guardrails by default.
MTU issue: An application has intermitte: Look for symptoms like large packets failing while small ones succeed, or TLS handshakes hanging - classic signs of an MTU mismatch causing fragmentation issues, especially over VPN tunnels which add overhead and reduce the effective MTU. Test with `ping -M do -s <size>` (or equivalent) to find the actual path MTU, and fix it by lowering the interface/tunnel MTU to match the path's actual capacity or enabling TCP MSS clamping so TCP negotiates a safe segment size automatically.
REST API automation: How would you desig: Design the API around explicit, validated resource requests (not arbitrary Terraform execution) with schema validation, quota checks, and policy checks applied before provisioning, and require the request to go through an approval step for anything above a defined risk threshold. Log every request and its outcome for audit, return clear errors when a request is rejected so users understand why, and version the API so you can evolve capabilities without breaking existing automation built on top of it.
Stakeholder tradeoff: Product wants fast: Reframe the conversation around shared outcomes rather than competing priorities - faster releases, fewer incidents, and strong security are not fundamentally opposed if the release process itself is safer (progressive delivery, automated gates, good observability) rather than slower. Use data (error budgets, incident trends, security findings) to make tradeoffs explicit and objective, and negotiate specific, time-boxed agreements (e.g. security gates automated within a quarter in exchange for faster manual review today) rather than a permanent standoff.
Platform team structure: How would you s: Structure around clear domains (infrastructure/networking, developer experience/golden paths, observability, security tooling) with each sub-team owning a specific part of the platform rather than one undifferentiated team trying to do everything. A commonly cited starting ratio is roughly 1 platform engineer per 8-12 product engineers, but the right ratio depends heavily on how much self-service automation exists - a more mature, automated platform can support a higher ratio since the team isn't doing manual work per request.
You-build-it-you-run-it: How would a pla: Provide golden-path templates that bake in observability, security controls, and deployment pipelines by default, so teams get production-grade tooling automatically when they use the platform rather than having to build it themselves. The platform team's job is to make the golden path the easiest option, not to mandate it - if opting out is harder than opting in, most teams will naturally consolidate on shared tooling instead of reinventing it.
Platform cost visibility: How would you: Enforce cost-allocation labels via policy so every resource is attributable to a team, and surface that data through existing tools rather than building new ones - a Looker Studio dashboard or a simple query against the billing export exposed in the internal developer portal, updated daily. Start with visibility (teams see their own spend trend) before adding budgets or enforcement, since visibility alone often drives meaningful behavior change without needing a full FinOps platform.
Golden path versioning: A golden path te: Version the golden path template explicitly (like a package version) so existing services stay pinned to their current version and are not silently broken by an update, and provide a clear, documented upgrade path for teams to opt into the new version on their own schedule. Track adoption via the version each service is running (surfaced in the service catalog), and proactively reach out to teams still on old versions if the update fixes something important like a security issue.
Platform onboarding: What would the firs: The engineer should be able to scaffold a new service from a golden path template with one command or a self-service UI, get a working CI/CD pipeline, observability, and a staging environment automatically, and deploy to production within their first day or two without filing a single infrastructure ticket. The experience should feel like the platform is doing the undifferentiated heavy lifting so the engineer's first contribution can be actual application logic, not YAML plumbing.
Platform team KPIs: What metrics would c: Track metrics that tie platform investment to business outcomes: time from idea to production deployment, percentage of infrastructure requests self-served versus ticket-driven, incident frequency/MTTR trend across teams using the platform versus those that aren't, and total engineering time saved by not duplicating infrastructure work per team. Frame these as the counterfactual cost of not having the platform - what it would cost if every team rebuilt this capability independently - since that's the comparison leadership actually cares about.
Platform API design: How would you versi: Use explicit API versioning (URL path or header-based) so breaking changes only affect consumers who opt into the new version, and prefer additive, backward-compatible changes (new optional fields, new endpoints) over modifying existing contracts whenever possible. Maintain a deprecation policy with a clear timeline and communication process for any version you do need to retire, and provide a way for consumers to discover which version they're on and what's changing before it affects them.
